items.label: Create
items.code:
a = []
a = [0] * 5
a = list(range(5))
items.note: list() converts any iterable to a list

items.label: Append & Extend
items.note: append adds one element; extend adds each element from iterable

items.label: Insert & Delete
items.code:
a.insert(1, 'x')   # insert at index 1
a.pop()             # remove & return last
a.pop(0)            # remove & return first
a.remove('x')       # remove first occurrence
del a[1]            # delete by index
items.note: pop(0) is O(n) — use deque for O(1) left pop

items.label: Slicing
items.code:
a[1:4]    # elements 1,2,3
a[::-1]   # reversed copy
a[::2]    # every 2nd element
a[-3:]    # last 3 elements
items.note: Slicing always returns a new list (shallow copy)

items.label: Sort vs Sorted
items.code:
a.sort()               # in-place, returns None
b = sorted(a)          # new list, a unchanged
a.sort(key=len)        # sort by length
a.sort(reverse=True)   # descending
items.note: .sort() returns None — don't assign it!

items.label: Useful Ops
items.code:
len(a)
min(a), max(a), sum(a)
a.index(val)    # first index of val
a.count(val)    # occurrences
3 in a          # membership O(n)
items.note: index() raises ValueError if not found

items.label: Copy Gotcha
items.code:
b = a           # b is SAME object
b = a[:]        # shallow copy
b = a.copy()    # shallow copy
import copy
b = copy.deepcopy(a)  # deep copy
items.note: Shallow copy only copies top level — nested lists still shared

items.label: 2D List Init
items.code:
# CORRECT
grid = [[0]*cols for _ in range(rows)]

# WRONG — rows share same inner list
grid = [[0]*cols] * rows
items.note: The * rows trap is the #1 Python gotcha in interviews

items.label: Create
items.note: Keys must be hashable (str, int, tuple — not list)

items.label: Get & Set
items.code:
d['a']            # KeyError if missing
d.get('a', 0)     # returns 0 if missing
d['c'] = 3        # set
d.setdefault('c', 0)  # set only if missing
items.note: setdefault returns existing value if key exists

items.label: Delete
items.code:
del d['a']              # KeyError if missing
val = d.pop('a')        # remove & return
val = d.pop('a', None)  # no error if missing
d.clear()               # remove all
items.note: pop with default is the safe way to remove

items.label: Iteration
items.code:
for k in d:             # keys
for k, v in d.items():  # key-value pairs
for v in d.values():    # values only
items.note: dict preserves insertion order (Python 3.7+)

items.label: defaultdict
items.code:
from collections import defaultdict
graph = defaultdict(list)
graph['a'].append('b')  # no KeyError

counts = defaultdict(int)
counts['x'] += 1        # starts at 0
items.note: Common: defaultdict(list) for graphs, defaultdict(int) for counting

items.label: Counter
items.code:
from collections import Counter
c = Counter('aabbc')     # {'a':2,'b':2,'c':1}
c.most_common(2)         # [('a',2),('b',2)]
c['z']                   # 0, no KeyError
c.update('aaa')          # add more counts
items.note: Counter supports +, -, &, | between counters

items.label: Dict Comprehension
items.note: Great for inverting dicts and filtering

items.label: Merge Dicts
items.code:
merged = {**d1, **d2}       # d2 wins conflicts
merged = d1 | d2            # Python 3.9+
d1.update(d2)               # in-place
items.note: | operator is the cleanest way in 3.9+

items.label: Create
items.note: {} creates an empty dict, not a set!

items.label: Add & Remove
items.code:
s.add(4)        # add element
s.discard(4)    # remove, no error if missing
s.remove(4)     # remove, KeyError if missing
s.pop()         # remove & return arbitrary elem
items.note: Use discard for safe removal

items.label: Set Operations
items.code:
a | b    # union
a & b    # intersection
a - b    # difference (in a but not b)
a ^ b    # symmetric diff (in one but not both)
items.note: All O(min(len(a),len(b))) — very fast

items.label: Subset & Superset
items.code:
a <= b    # a is subset of b
a < b     # a is proper subset
a >= b    # a is superset of b
a.isdisjoint(b)  # no common elements
items.note: Useful for checking containment relationships

items.label: Membership
items.code:
if x in s:    # O(1) average

# Convert list to set for fast lookups
word_set = set(word_list)
if word in word_set:  # O(1) vs O(n)
items.note: This is THE reason to use sets — O(1) lookup

items.label: Set Comprehension
items.code:
evens = {x for x in range(10) if x % 2 == 0}
unique_lens = {len(w) for w in words}
items.note: Same syntax as dict comp but without key:value

items.label: Frozenset
items.code:
fs = frozenset([1, 2, 3])  # immutable set
d = {fs: 'value'}          # can be dict key
s.add(frozenset([4, 5]))   # set of sets
items.note: Use frozenset when you need a hashable set

items.label: Immutable
items.code:
s = 'hello'
# s[0] = 'H'  # TypeError!
s = 'H' + s[1:]  # create new string
items.note: Strings cannot be modified in place — build new ones

items.label: Slicing
items.code:
s[1:4]      # 'ell'
s[::-1]     # 'olleh' (reverse)
s[::2]      # 'hlo' (every 2nd char)
items.note: Same slicing rules as lists

items.label: Split & Join
items.code:
words = s.split()         # split on whitespace
parts = s.split(',')      # split on comma
' '.join(words)           # join with space
''.join(reversed(s))      # reverse string
items.note: join is called on the separator, not the list

items.label: Strip & Check
items.code:
s.strip()       # remove leading/trailing whitespace
s.lstrip('0')   # remove leading zeros
s.isdigit()     # all digits?
s.isalpha()     # all letters?
s.isalnum()     # letters or digits?
s.lower(), s.upper()
items.note: strip/lstrip/rstrip remove characters, not substrings

items.label: Find & Replace
items.code:
s.find('lo')      # 3  (index, -1 if not found)
s.index('lo')     # 3  (raises ValueError if not found)
s.count('l')      # 2
s.replace('l', 'L')  # 'heLLo'
s.startswith('he')   # True
items.note: find returns -1, index raises — know the difference

items.label: ord & chr
items.code:
ord('a')          # 97
chr(97)           # 'a'
ord('a') - ord('A')  # 32

# char to 0-25 index
i = ord(c) - ord('a')
items.note: Essential for bucket counting and char math

items.label: String Building
items.code:
# Efficient: join a list
parts = []
for c in s:
    parts.append(c.upper())
result = ''.join(parts)

# f-strings
f'{name}: {score:.2f}'
items.note: Don't concat in a loop (s += c) — O(n^2). Use join.

items.label: Common Patterns
items.code:
# Check palindrome
s == s[::-1]

# Count chars
from collections import Counter
Counter(s)

# Is anagram
Counter(s1) == Counter(s2)
items.note: These come up constantly in interviews

items.label: Create
items.code:
t = ()
t = (1, 2, 3)
t = tuple([1, 2, 3])  # from iterable
items.note: Tuples are immutable sequences

items.label: Single Element
items.code:
t = (1,)    # single element — comma required!
t = (1)     # this is just the int 1, NOT a tuple
type((1,))  # <class 'tuple'>
type((1))   # <class 'int'>
items.note: Forgetting the trailing comma is a classic bug

items.label: Unpacking
items.code:
a, b, c = (1, 2, 3)
first, *rest = [1, 2, 3, 4]   # first=1, rest=[2,3,4]
_, val, _ = (1, 2, 3)         # ignore with _
items.note: * unpacking works with any iterable

items.label: Swap
items.code:
a, b = b, a     # Pythonic swap
# Works because right side is evaluated first
# as a tuple, then unpacked
items.note: No temp variable needed — use this in interviews

items.label: As Dict Key / Set Element
items.code:
d = {}
d[(1, 2)] = 'point'     # tuple as key
seen = set()
seen.add((r, c))        # tuple in set

# lists are NOT hashable
# d[[1,2]] = 'x'  # TypeError!
items.note: Tuples are hashable (if contents are) — lists are not

items.label: Named Tuple
items.code:
from collections import namedtuple
Point = namedtuple('Point', ['x', 'y'])
p = Point(1, 2)
p.x, p.y  # 1, 2
items.note: Readable alternative to plain tuples

items.label: Tuple Comparison
items.code:
# Compares element by element
(1, 2) < (1, 3)     # True
(1, 2) < (2, 0)     # True

# Great for sorting by multiple keys
intervals.sort(key=lambda x: (x[0], -x[1]))
items.note: Lexicographic comparison — crucial for priority queues

items.label: Stack (list)
items.code:
stack = []
stack.append(1)    # push
stack.append(2)
top = stack[-1]    # peek
val = stack.pop()  # pop (LIFO)
len(stack) == 0    # is empty
items.note: list works perfectly as a stack — all O(1)

items.label: Queue (deque)
items.code:
from collections import deque
q = deque()
q.append(1)       # enqueue (right)
val = q.popleft() # dequeue (left) O(1)
q[0]              # peek front
items.note: Never use list as queue — pop(0) is O(n)

items.label: Deque Methods
items.code:
d = deque([1, 2, 3])
d.append(4)        # add right
d.appendleft(0)    # add left
d.pop()            # remove right
d.popleft()        # remove left
d.rotate(1)        # [3, 0, 1, 2]
items.note: deque is doubly-linked — O(1) on both ends

items.label: Monotonic Stack
items.code:
# Next Greater Element pattern
result = [-1] * len(nums)
stack = []  # stores indices
for i, num in enumerate(nums):
    while stack and nums[stack[-1]] < num:
        result[stack.pop()] = num
    stack.append(i)
items.note: Stack holds indices of elements waiting for a greater value

items.label: BFS Pattern
items.note: BFS always uses a queue — gives shortest path in unweighted graphs

items.label: BFS with Levels
items.note: The for-range-len(q) trick processes exactly one level at a time

items.label: Stack for DFS
items.note: Replace deque+popleft with list+pop to convert BFS to DFS

items.label: Basics
items.code:
import heapq
h = []
heapq.heappush(h, 3)
heapq.heappush(h, 1)
val = heapq.heappop(h)   # 1 (smallest)
peek = h[0]              # peek min, no pop
items.note: Python heapq is a MIN heap — smallest comes out first

items.label: Heapify
items.code:
nums = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5]
heapq.heapify(nums)     # O(n), in-place
# nums is now a valid heap
items.note: heapify is O(n), faster than n pushes O(n log n)

items.label: Max-Heap Trick
items.code:
# Negate values to simulate max-heap
heapq.heappush(h, -val)
max_val = -heapq.heappop(h)
items.note: Always negate on push AND pop — easy to forget one

items.label: Tuple Priority
items.code:
# (priority, data) — sorts by first element
heapq.heappush(h, (dist, node))
heapq.heappush(h, (freq, word))

# Tie-breaking: add counter
heapq.heappush(h, (priority, counter, item))
items.note: If priorities tie and items aren't comparable, add a counter

items.label: N Largest / Smallest
items.code:
heapq.nlargest(3, nums)              # top 3
heapq.nsmallest(3, nums)             # bottom 3
heapq.nlargest(3, d, key=d.get)      # top 3 keys by value
items.note: More efficient than sorting when k << n

items.label: Top-K Pattern
items.note: Size-k heap approach: O(n log k) — great for streaming data

items.label: Merge Sorted Lists
items.code:
merged = list(heapq.merge(l1, l2, l3))
# Lazily merges sorted iterables
items.note: heapq.merge is lazy — great for merging k sorted lists

items.label: Dijkstra Pattern
items.note: Classic shortest path — skip stale entries with the dist check

items.label: for & range
items.note: range is lazy — doesn't create a list in memory

items.label: enumerate
items.code:
for i, val in enumerate(arr):       # i=0,1,...
for i, val in enumerate(arr, 1):    # i=1,2,...
items.note: Always prefer enumerate over range(len(arr))

items.label: zip
items.code:
for a, b in zip(list1, list2):  # parallel iteration

# Unzip
pairs = [(1,'a'), (2,'b')]
nums, chars = zip(*pairs)  # (1,2), ('a','b')
items.note: zip stops at shortest. Use zip_longest for full coverage.

items.label: zip_longest
items.note: Essential when lists have different lengths

items.label: reversed & sorted
items.note: reversed() is lazy and doesn't create a copy

items.label: while & break
items.note: for/while else: else block runs when NO break occurred

items.label: itertools Essentials
items.code:
from itertools import product, permutations, combinations
list(product('AB', '12'))    # [('A','1'),('A','2'),...]
list(permutations([1,2,3]))  # all orderings
list(combinations([1,2,3], 2))  # all pairs
items.note: product = nested loops; combinations = without replacement

items.label: Multiple Assignment in Loop
items.note: Direction array pattern is essential for grid problems

items.label: List Comprehension
items.code:
squares = [x**2 for x in range(10)]
evens = [x for x in nums if x % 2 == 0]
items.note: Faster and more Pythonic than append loops

items.label: With Condition
items.code:
# Filter
pos = [x for x in nums if x > 0]

# Transform + Filter
upper = [c.upper() for c in s if c.isalpha()]

# If-else (no filter, maps all)
labels = ['even' if x%2==0 else 'odd' for x in nums]
items.note: if at end = filter. if-else before for = transform.

items.label: Dict Comprehension
items.note: Last value wins for duplicate keys

items.label: Set Comprehension
items.code:
unique_lens = {len(w) for w in words}
vowels = {c for c in s if c in 'aeiou'}
items.note: Like list comp but with {} — auto-deduplicates

items.label: Nested Comprehension
items.code:
# Flatten 2D list
flat = [x for row in matrix for x in row]

# Equivalent to:
flat = []
for row in matrix:
    for x in row:
        flat.append(x)
items.note: Read nested comps left to right, same as nested for loops

items.label: Generator Expression
items.code:
total = sum(x**2 for x in range(1000))
any_neg = any(x < 0 for x in nums)
all_pos = all(x > 0 for x in nums)
items.note: Generators are lazy — no intermediate list in memory

items.label: 2D Matrix Init
items.code:
# Create m x n matrix of zeros
grid = [[0] * n for _ in range(m)]

# Create adjacency list
adj = [[] for _ in range(n)]
items.note: Always use comprehension for 2D init, never [[0]*n]*m

items.label: sorted vs .sort()
items.code:
b = sorted(a)     # new list, a unchanged
a.sort()           # in-place, returns None

# sorted works on any iterable
sorted('hello')    # ['e','h','l','l','o']
sorted({3,1,2})    # [1,2,3]
items.note: .sort() is list-only and returns None. sorted() works on anything.

items.label: Key Function
items.code:
words.sort(key=len)                   # by length
words.sort(key=str.lower)             # case insensitive
points.sort(key=lambda p: p[1])       # by y-coord
points.sort(key=lambda p: (p[0], -p[1]))  # multi-key
items.note: key function is called once per element, result used for comparison

items.label: Reverse
items.code:
a.sort(reverse=True)         # descending
sorted(a, reverse=True)

# Negate for partial reverse in tuples
a.sort(key=lambda x: (x[0], -x[1]))  # asc by [0], desc by [1]
items.note: Negate trick only works for numbers, not strings

items.label: Tuple Sorting
items.code:
pairs = [(2,'b'), (1,'a'), (2,'a')]
sorted(pairs)  # [(1,'a'), (2,'a'), (2,'b')]
# Sorts by first element, then second on ties
items.note: Tuples compare lexicographically — great for multi-key sorting

items.label: Stable Sort
items.code:
# Python sort is stable: equal elements keep
# their original order. This means you can:

# Sort by secondary key first, then primary
data.sort(key=lambda x: x[1])   # secondary
data.sort(key=lambda x: x[0])   # primary
items.note: Stability lets you do multi-pass sorts safely

items.label: cmp_to_key
items.code:
from functools import cmp_to_key
def compare(a, b):
    if a + b > b + a: return -1
    elif a + b < b + a: return 1
    else: return 0
nums_str = sorted(strs, key=cmp_to_key(compare))
items.note: Use for custom comparators (e.g., LC 179 Largest Number)

items.label: Sort by Index / Value
items.code:
# Sort indices by their values
idx = sorted(range(len(a)), key=lambda i: a[i])

# Sort dict by value
sorted(d.items(), key=lambda x: x[1])
items.note: Sorting indices is useful when you need original positions

items.label: min / max with key
items.code:
min(words, key=len)           # shortest word
max(d, key=d.get)             # key with max value
min(nums, key=abs)            # closest to zero
items.note: key param avoids writing verbose loops

items.label: any / all
items.code:
any(x > 0 for x in nums)   # True if ANY positive
all(x > 0 for x in nums)   # True if ALL positive
any(c.isdigit() for c in s) # has any digit?
items.note: Short-circuit: any stops at first True, all at first False

items.label: sum
items.code:
sum(nums)
sum(nums, start=10)          # 10 + sum
sum(row[0] for row in matrix) # sum of first col
items.note: Don't use sum for string concat — use ''.join()

items.label: divmod
items.code:
q, r = divmod(17, 5)   # (3, 2)

# Convert seconds to min:sec
m, s = divmod(total_seconds, 60)

# Convert 1D index to 2D
r, c = divmod(index, cols)
items.note: divmod(a,b) = (a//b, a%b) — one call instead of two

items.label: bisect
items.code:
import bisect
i = bisect.bisect_left(arr, x)   # leftmost insert pos
i = bisect.bisect_right(arr, x)  # rightmost insert pos
bisect.insort(arr, x)            # insert & keep sorted
items.note: O(log n) search on sorted arrays — know left vs right

items.label: math Module
items.code:
import math
math.inf, -math.inf    # infinity
math.ceil(3.2)         # 4
math.floor(3.8)        # 3
math.gcd(12, 8)        # 4
math.log2(8)           # 3.0
math.sqrt(16)          # 4.0
items.note: float('inf') also works and is more common in LC

items.label: map / filter
items.code:
nums = list(map(int, input().split()))
pos = list(filter(lambda x: x > 0, nums))

# But list comps are usually cleaner:
nums = [int(x) for x in input().split()]
items.note: List comprehensions are preferred over map/filter in interviews

items.label: abs / pow / isinstance
items.code:
abs(-5)             # 5
pow(2, 10)          # 1024
pow(2, 10, MOD)     # (2^10) % MOD — fast modpow
isinstance(x, int)  # type check
isinstance(x, (int, float))  # multiple types
items.note: 3-arg pow for modular exponentiation is very handy

items.label: 2D List Trap
items.code:
# WRONG — all rows are the SAME list
grid = [[0] * 3] * 3
grid[0][0] = 1  # changes ALL rows!

# RIGHT
grid = [[0] * 3 for _ in range(3)]
items.note: This is the #1 most common Python interview bug

items.label: Mutable Default Args
items.note: Default mutable args are created ONCE, not per call

items.label: is vs ==
items.code:
a = [1, 2]
b = [1, 2]
a == b   # True (same value)
a is b   # False (different objects)

# Use 'is' only for None, True, False
if x is None:
items.note: 'is' checks identity (same object), '==' checks equality

items.label: Integer Division
items.code:
7 // 2     #  3
-7 // 2    # -4  (rounds toward -inf, NOT toward 0!)

# To truncate toward zero like C/Java:
int(-7 / 2)  # -3
items.note: Python // rounds toward negative infinity — catches many people

items.label: Shallow Copy
items.code:
a = [[1, 2], [3, 4]]
b = a[:]           # shallow copy
b[0][0] = 99       # a is ALSO modified!

import copy
b = copy.deepcopy(a)  # fully independent
items.note: Shallow copy only copies the outer list, not nested objects

items.label: in: List O(n) vs Set O(1)
items.code:
# SLOW — O(n) per lookup
if x in large_list:    # scans entire list

# FAST — O(1) per lookup
large_set = set(large_list)
if x in large_set:
items.note: Converting to set is O(n) once, then O(1) per lookup

items.label: .sort() Returns None
items.code:
# WRONG
sorted_list = my_list.sort()  # sorted_list is None!

# RIGHT — either:
my_list.sort()                # in-place
sorted_list = sorted(my_list) # new list
items.note: In-place methods (.sort, .reverse, .append) return None

items.label: String Concatenation in Loop
items.code:
# SLOW — O(n^2)
s = ''
for c in chars:
    s += c

# FAST — O(n)
s = ''.join(chars)
items.note: Each += creates a new string. Use join for O(n).

items.label: Modifying List While Iterating
items.note: Never modify a list you're iterating over
